chunk_summary: Inpatient stabilization for complicated SAM includes triage for complications, cautious rehydration, electrolyte correction, therapeutic feeding, and antibiotic regimens tailored to severity and local protocols.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of antibiotics in managing complicated SAM during inpatient stabilization.
Learn the triage priorities for complications in children with SAM, including hypoglycemia and dehydration.
Identify appropriate antibiotic regimens for complicated SAM and criteria for transitioning to oral therapy.
Recognize the importance of documenting key decision points in SAM management pathways.
key_facts: Children with SAM often have impaired immune responses and may present with serious bacterial infections without classic signs.
Empiric parenteral antibiotic regimen: ampicillin 50 mg/kg every 6 hours plus gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg once daily.
Step down to oral amoxicillin 25–40 mg/kg every 8 hours for approximately 5 days once the child stabilizes.
Persistent fever requires evaluation for pneumonia, urinary infection, malaria, or TB risk rather than repeated empiric antibiotics.
Uncomplicated SAM managed outpatient often includes a short course of oral amoxicillin to reduce mortality and clinical deterioration.
reference_quote: Antibiotics are part of stabilization for complicated SAM, with empiric parenteral regimens typically including ampicillin and gentamicin, followed by oral amoxicillin as the child stabilizes. (WHO eLENA)
document_key_topics: Management of complicated SAM with antibiotics
Triage and stabilization priorities in inpatient care
Guidelines for transitioning from parenteral to oral antibiotics
